data.title: ADNOC Gas PLC Announces Dividend for the Third Quarter of 2025, Payable on December 12, 2025
data.description:
ADNOC Gas PLC announced dividend for the third quarter of 2025. The company is introducing quarterly dividend distributions starting Third Quarter 2025, with an inaugural Third Quarter interim dividend of $896 million to be paid by December 12, 2025. Ex-Dividend date is November 21, 2025.

Record date is November 24, 2025.

data.title: JD.com, Inc. Reports Earnings Results for the Third Quarter and Nine Months Ended September 30, 2025
data.description:
JD.com, Inc. reported earnings results for the third quarter and nine months ended September 30, 2025. For the third quarter, the company reported revenue was CNY 299,059 million compared to CNY 260,387 million a year ago. Net income was CNY 5,276 million compared to CNY 11,731 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 3.72 compared to CNY 8.04 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 3.38 compared to CNY 7.72 a year ago.
For the nine months, revenue was CNY 956,801 million compared to CNY 811,833 million a year ago. Net income was CNY 22,344 million compared to CNY 31,505 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 15.62 compared to CNY 20.88 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 14.78 compared to CNY 20.38 a year ago.

data.title: Jubilant FoodWorks Limited Reports Earnings Results for the Second Quarter and Six Months Ended September 30, 2025
data.description:
Jubilant FoodWorks Limited reported earnings results for the second quarter and six months ended September 30, 2025. For the second quarter, the company reported sales was INR 23,401.52 million compared to INR 19,547.16 million a year ago. Revenue was INR 23,553.81 million compared to INR 19,849.3 million a year ago. Net income was INR 1,860.31 million compared to INR 640.46 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 1.61 compared to INR 1.05 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 1.61 compared to INR 1.05 a year ago.
For the six months, sales was INR 46,010.12 million compared to INR 38,877.8 million a year ago. Revenue was INR 46,347.75 million compared to INR 39,351.25 million a year ago. Net income was INR 2,777.87 million compared to INR 1,198.46 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 3.04 compared to INR 1.93 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 3.04 compared to INR 1.93 a year ago.

data.title: Brookfield Corporation Declares Quarterly Dividend, Payable on December 31, 2025
data.description: Brookfield Corporation declared a quarterly dividend for company of $0.06 per share, payable on December 31, 2025 to shareholders of record as at the close of business on December 16, 2025.

data.title: Duncan Regional Hospital Becomes First in U.S. to Implement BD Alaris EMR Infusion Interoperability with MEDITECH
data.description:
BD (Becton, Dickinson and Company) announced that Duncan Regional Hospital ("DRH Health") has become the first hospital in the United States to implement BD Alaris?? EMR (Electronic Medical Record) Interoperability with the MEDITECH electronic health record (EHR), marking a significant advancement in patient safety, workflow efficiency and clinician support. This integration brings together BD's robust market-leading bi-directional infusion interoperability - now live at more than 960 sites in the United States.

By expanding this capability to MEDITECH, BD now offers its Alaris?? EMR Interoperability solution to all three of the leading EHR systems in the U.S. market, providing advanced integration to an even broader range of healthcare organizations nationwide. Enhancing Patient Safety and Efficiency: BD Alaris??

E MR Interoperability enables seamless connection of infusion pumps into MEDITECH's Patient Care and Patient Safety solution. Clinicians can now scan to verify patients, medications and smart pumps; send infusion orders directly from MEDITECH to BD Alaris?? pumps; and receive infusion statuses back into the EHR.

By reducing manual programming and error-prone steps, this solution supports comprehensive, near real-time documentation, and helps ensure the right patient receives the right medication at the right dose while improving safety across the board. By seamlessly connecting infusion pumps to EMR systems, the solution helps decrease the risk of IV medication errors and extends safety to every infusion administered.

data.title: Hydro One Limited Declares Quarterly Common Share Dividend, Payable on December 31, 2025
data.description: Hydro One Limited announced that its Board of Directors has declared a quarterly cash dividend to common shareholders of CAD 0.3331 per share to be paid on December 31, 2025 to shareholders of record on December 10, 2025.

data.title: Tata Steel calls for extension of import tariffs on some steel products to India
data.description:
NEW DELHI (Reuters) -India's Tata Steel hopes the government will extend import tariffs on some steel products to guard against the threat of imports, particularly from China, chief executive T.V. Narendran told Reuters on Thursday.

"Import volumes are limited in India but even small volumes disturb the market," said Narendran in an interview.

"China is exporting a lot of steel," globally Narendran said, adding that Indian steel producers did not have an option to export because most markets were "flooded" by Chinese competitors.

India, the world's second-biggest crude steel producer, had in April imposed temporary import tariffs on some steel products to curb imports from China. Those tariffs expired last week.

Meanwhile, Narendran said that Tata Steel was impacted by U.S. tariffs on the company's Europe and UK operations, which had been exporting a combined 800,000 metric tons to the U.S. Some of the impact was passed on to customers, he said.

The steel producer is looking at alternative markets such as Latin America, parts of Europe, Africa and the Middle East to deal with the impact of U.S. tariffs, Narendran said.

Tata Steel is also urging the UK government to impose import curbs to stem cheap shipments into the country, Koushik Chatterjee, chief financial officer at Tata Steel said.

"UK has become literally a dumping ground for imports," Chatterjee said.

As for costs related to its European operations, the steel producer is in the process of finalising the total sum of expenditure required to decarbonise its plant in the Netherlands. Job cuts the company announced in April in the Netherlands have also yet to take effect, Chatterjee said.

"That is something which is taking time because we are in negotiation with the union," Chatterjee said.

INDIA OUTLOOK

Tata Steel expects steel prices in Q3 will be 1,500 rupees lower per ton than Q2 in India.

Demand in India is "quite strong", particularly from automotives, oil and gas, railways and the construction sector, Narendran said.

(Reporting by Neha Arora and Sethuraman N R; Editing by Ronojoy Mazumdar)

By Neha Arora and Sethuraman N R

data.title: Palatin fiscal Q1 revenue hits $8.8 mln, net income $4.7 mln
Nokia Completes Directed Share Issue to Nvidia
data.description:
Telecommunications company Nokia has finalized the previously announced directed share issue to U.S. chip manufacturer Nvidia, according to a press release.

The transaction was initially announced on October 28. Nvidia is investing $1 billion in Nokia and will receive 166.39 million new shares, corresponding to a 2.90 percent ownership stake. Following this transaction, Nokia will have a total of 5.74 billion shares outstanding.

data.title: FTSE 100 retreats as financial and energy stocks drag, GDP data disappoints
data.description:
(Reuters) -London's FTSE 100 fell on Thursday after a three-day run of record highs, dragged by financial and energy shares, as investors assessed disappointing third-quarter economic growth data.

The blue-chip index was down 0.6% as of 12:11 GMT, though it remained within reach of the significant 10,000 points milestone.

The mid-cap FTSE 250 also dipped 0.3%.

Investment banks & brokerages dropped 6.3%, as investment firm 3i Group slumped 14.6%, set for its worst day since May 2022, after the company said it is cautious in deployment of capital into new investment.

Energy sector declined 1.1% after a report showing rising crude inventories in the U.S. reinforced oversupply concerns, bringing down oil prices.

Britain's economic performance showed minimal momentum in the third quarter, with growth hampered by a cyber attack on Jaguar Land Rover in September.

This lacklustre showing reinforced market expectations for an interest rate cut from the Bank of England in December. Finance Minister Rachel Reeves' budget announcement is scheduled for November 26.

Sterling briefly touched a session low following the data release before recovering to trade 0.2% higher.

Global markets remained focused on the flow of economic data from the U.S. after President Donald Trump signed legislation ending the country's longest government shutdown. The 43-day data blackout left both the Federal Reserve and traders uncertain about labour market conditions and inflation trends.

Life insurers fell 1.3% on Thursday, as Aviva dropped 4.3% after the insurer's new financial targets failed to impress investors. 

But precious metal miners climbed 8.2% as gold prices hit a more than three-week high.

Homebuilder Persimmon rose 2.3% after reporting a rise in its forward sales despite consumer uncertainty.

Burberry shares rose 2.4%, hitting their highest since July 29, after the luxury brand posted its first quarter of growth in two years.

Wizz Air's shares climbed 8.7% after the airline reported strong first-half operating profit.

(Reporting by Utkarsh Tushar Hathi; Editing by Sahal Muhammed)

Siemens Expects Currency Headwinds to Weigh on Results -- Update
data.description:
By Nina Kienle


Siemens said it expects currency headwinds to heavily weigh on its results for the year ahead, as the industrial group moves to spin off a big chunk of its $39 billion stake in medical-equipment supplier Healthineers.

The German conglomerate has sought to simplify its sprawling operations in recent years, while making acquisitions to boost its software portfolio and tap into demand driven by artificial intelligence and the electrification of everything from cars to homes.

Shares in Siemens have climbed to record highs this year as the company benefited from AI spending and the prospect of higher infrastructure investment in its home market, but its stock fell Thursday after the company issued a downbeat outlook.

Siemens said currency effects would hurt its volume growth rates, as well as profit from industrial businesses, as it issued guidance for the year ending in September 2026 that fell short of consensus expectations.

The company is working to integrate recently acquired Altair and Dotmatics, which it hopes will help expand its reach in software and AI. It said the planned deconsolidation of Siemens Healthineers sharpens the group's focus on its core portfolio.

Siemens said it would distribute to shareholders a 30% stake in Siemens Healthineers, out of a holding of roughly 67% valued at about 33.9 billion euros ($39.30 billion), with a view to reducing its stake to a financial asset in the medium term.

Shares in Siemens were down as much as 5.5% in European midday trading Thursday, dropping from a record closing high hit Wednesday, but were still up by about a quarter since the start of the year.

The deconsolidation process for Siemens Healthineers is set to be lengthier than anticipated and the tax implications remain unclear since this is an unprecedented transaction in Germany, analysts at Deutsche Bank wrote in a note to clients. The news came alongside disappointing guidance for fiscal 2026 and mixed results, they added.

Siemens said it anticipates earnings per shares before purchase price allocation accounting in a range of 10.40 euros to 11.00 euros in fiscal 2026. The range falls short of the market expectation of 11.54 euros, according to a company compiled consensus.

Meanwhile, fourth-quarter revenue amounted to 21.43 billion euros, rising from 20.81 billion euros. The figure came in line with analysts' forecast of 21.43 billion euros, according to estimates provided by the company.

Profit from industrial business rose to 3.19 billion euros from 3.12 billion euros, with a narrowed margin of 15.3% from 15.5%.

The company proposed a dividend of 5.35 euros a share, up from 5.20 euros a share.


Write to Nina Kienle at nina.kienle@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 0733ET

Nordea Bank Accidentally Sends List of Customers' Names and Personal Identification Numbers
data.description:
Nordea Bank has mistakenly sent a list containing the names and personal identification numbers of 8,600 of its customers to 1,400 other customers in Norway, according to the Norwegian newspaper VG.

"A regrettable error has occurred, through which a list with 8,600 names was sent to 1,400 customers in Innlandet early this morning," wrote Communications Director Christian Steffensen in an email.

The incident concerns local customers who were supposed to be invited to an event.

"This was a serious human error, for which we apologize," Steffensen said.

data.title: Atlanta Fed's Bostic to Retire; How Economists Are Dealing With the Data Drought
data.description:
Atlanta Fed's Raphael Bostic to Retire; How Economists Are Dealing With the Data Drought By Michael Maloney

Atlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic said he would retire when his current five-year term expires at the end of next February. His decision to retire now comes as the Fed's seven-member board in Washington must agree to the reappointment of all 12 of the system's bank presidents to new five year terms beginning March 1. Meanwhile, during the six-week government shutdown, professional economic forecasters have learned a few things in the data wilderness, like how to sleep past 5 a.m. and what the plumber can tell you about Americans' spending. Now that President Trump has signed legislation to fund the government, the return to normal is expected to come relatively quickly-though mountains of work awaits federal workers. The White House has indicated, however, that inflation and employment reports for last month will be lost due to the long closure of federal agencies.

Top News Atlanta Fed's Bostic to Retire Ahead of Reappointment Process

Raphael Bostic became the first African-American and openly gay president

of a regional Fed bank in its history when he took the job as Atlanta Fed leader in 2017. He faced scrutiny three years ago after he disclosed he had failed to adhere to rules governing senior officials' personal-financial transactions, but retained the support of his board of directors at the time. Bostic, now 59, was eligible to serve as the bank's president for another six years until hitting a mandatory retirement age of 65.

Fed's Collins Sees 'High Bar' for Further Rate Cuts

Boston Fed President Susan Collins said Wednesday there should be a "high bar" for further easing

by the Federal Reserve in the months ahead, projecting that the Fed should likely "keep policy rates at the current level for some time."

Inside Economists' Search for Meaning During the Shutdown

For the past six weeks during a government data drought, economists have searched for meaning

wherever they could find it. For some, that's meant tracking obscure metrics like visits to the Statue of Liberty. Others used the government shutdown to dust off esoteric research projects. One economist said he asked his plumber how tariffs are affecting his business when he came to do work in his home.

Trump Signs Spending Bill to Reopen the Government

The GOP-led House passed a spending package reopening the government

and President Trump signed it into law late Wednesday, drawing to a close a record-long 43-day shutdown driven by Democrats' demands to extend expiring healthcare subsides.

White House Says October Jobs, Inflation Reports Unlikely to Be Released

The Secret Meeting That Ended the Shutdown

What to Know as the Government Reopens

Breaking Down the Shutdown Deal, From Back Pay to Hemp Provisions

Economy Builders' Cheap Mortgages Are a Bad Deal for Home Buyers

Bargain mortgages originated by builders are helping more people get onto the property ladder. The hidden cost is inflated home values and underwater loans .

Yen Weakens After First Face-Off Between Japan PM, BOJ Governor

The first face-to-face meeting between Japan's prime minister and the head of its central bank has cooled expectations that an interest-rate hike is coming soon , sending the yen to a nine-month low.

U.K. Economy Grows at Slower Pace Ahead of Budget

U.K. economic growth continued to slow in the third quarter

amid uncertainty about the government's coming budget and the impact of a cyberattack on a major carmaker.

Britain Is Preparing Tens of Billions in New Taxes-Again

Eurozone Industry Ekes Out Growth, Dragged by Ireland

The eurozone's industry returned to slow growth in September , reflecting moderate resilience at a time of trade uncertainty and despite a significant drag in the month from volatile Irish data.

OPEC Holds Oil-Demand Forecast Steady

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries left its oil-demand forecasts unchanged

as it continues to raise production through the end of the year, while also pointing to rising global oil inventories.

Financial Regulation Coinbase to Leave Delaware, Reincorporate in Texas

Coinbase plans to leave Delaware and reincorporate in Texas

after a group of shareholders with a majority of voting power voted to approve the move, the company said in a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paul Grewal said in an opinion piece in The Wall Street Journal that the company is pursuing the reincorporation because Texas' legal framework is more predictable and efficient than Delaware's.

Forward Guidance Thursday (all times ET)

7 a.m.: Bank of England's Megan Greene speaks at Chatham House event on central bank independence

8:30 a.m.: U.S. weekly initial jobless claims

12:20 p.m.: Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack speaks at Economic Club of Pittsburgh fireside chat

12:30 p.m.: St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem speaks at Evansville Regional Economic Partnership event

Friday

5 a.m.: EU third-quarter GDP flash estimate

Basis Points Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the Trump administration would take action to help lower

the price of coffee, bananas and other products grown abroad. A major shopping event in China showed sales improved , but not by enough to dispel concerns that tepid consumer spending will continue to drag on the economy. About Us

WSJ Pro Central Banking brings you central banking news, analysis and insights from WSJ's global team of reporters and editors. This newsletter was compiled by Michael Maloney in New York and Nihad Ahmed in Barcelona.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 0731ET

EU Investigates Alphabet's Google over Publishers' Ranking Policy -- Update
data.description:
By Edith Hancock


The European Commission opened a new investigation into Alphabet's Google over how the tech giant ranks news publishers in search results.

The commission said Thursday that it is concerned that Google might be demoting news media and other publishers' websites and content in search results when they include content from third-party commercial partners. The European Union's executive arm said Google's so-called site reputation abuse policy could impede publishers from legitimately making money online through third-party content.

The investigation is being opened under the Digital Markets Act. The DMA sets out obligations for a handful of tech giants that aim to make it easier for businesses that rely on their widely popular platforms-- such as search engines and app stores--to reach customers.

Companies can face fines of up to 10% of their annual worldwide turnover if the EU decides they are breaking the DMA.

"We will investigate to ensure that news publishers are not losing out on important revenues at a difficult time for the industry, and to ensure Google complies with the Digital Markets Act," Teresa Ribera, the bloc's competition commissioner, said.

Google said in a blog post Thursday that its ranking-related policy is essential to fight deceptive pay-for-play tactics, saying those practices degrade its own results. It said the policy tackles "parasite SEO", where spammers pay more reputable publishers to show their content on the publishers' website to leverage their better standing and climb higher on search results.

"The investigation announced today into our anti-spam efforts is misguided and risks harming millions of European users," Pandu Nayak, chief scientist of Google Search, said in a blog post.

Enforcement of the DMA comes despite criticism from the U.S. in recent months, which has sought to bring the law into broader trade talks between the two regions. President Trump has hit out at Brussels' antitrust actions against Big Tech, saying they amount to a tax on successful American companies, and threatened to impose tariffs on countries that crack down on U.S. tech groups.

The commission fined Google 2.95 billion euros ($3.42 billion) in September after a years-long investigation into its advertising technology business, prompting Trump to call the move "very unfair" and "discriminatory" in a Truth Social post.

The search giant's AI Overviews have also stirred up criticism from publishers, who had complained to antitrust regulators in the EU and U.K. earlier this year that the company was unfairly directing traffic away from news websites.

Google had responded at the time that AI tools in search create new opportunities for businesses to get visibility.


Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 0730ET
